narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.30 and an LSAT score of 40—both above the typical averages for students in our dataset—your law school application stood out in several ways, including your upper-middle family income background and full-time study plans. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 59.6% probability that you would fail it, tipping the scales toward rejection to minimize the risk of poor bar passage outcomes for the program. This prediction was driven primarily by three key factors from your profile, ranked by their influence on the decision. The most influential was your undergraduate GPA; even though it's slightly above average at 3.30, it pushed the model toward predicting failure because GPAs in this range have historically correlated with higher bar failure rates among similar students. Next, your Year 3 school decile ranking of 9—well above the average of around 5.5—strongly pulled in the opposite direction, suggesting a likelihood of passing based on that late-stage academic strength. Your LSAT score, while strong at 40 compared to the average of 36.7, still nudged the prediction slightly toward failure, as the model weighs it as a modest positive signal but not enough to fully offset other concerns. Overall, your profile compares favorably to many applicants in LSAT and decile performance, but the model saw your GPA as the biggest red flag relative to students who typically pass the bar. These factors combined to create just enough doubt about first-try bar success, leading to the denial. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to push it even higher or bolstering your GPA narrative with explanations of upward trends, like your impressive Year 3 decile. Strengthening letters of recommendation that highlight your full-time commitment and family-supported drive could also help in reapplying. Many students in your position improve their profiles and succeed on appeal or at other programs—your above-average markers show real potential. Focus on targeted prep for the bar predictors, and you'll likely shift that probability in your favor next time.
